title: A Reply, or an Exercise in Blame? (2)
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The printed source was checked for several severe OCR intrusions. It reads `தற்போது தேர்தல் வருகிறது` ("now that an election is approaching"), `ஈர்த்திழுத்திடும்` ("draw and attract"), and `110வது விதியின் கீழ்` ("under Rule 110"). These and only source-confirmed or unambiguous OCR/page-break repairs are restored in the Tamil reading copy. The canonical Tamil chapter remains unchanged.
paragraphs: At the very outset of her reply to the debate on the Governor's Address, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa said: "Although there was an opportunity to express views in the Legislative Assembly, some people failed to use it and, driven only by a desire to heap accusations upon the government, spoke irresponsibly outside as they pleased." Is an opportunity actually provided in the Assembly to express views democratically and within its rules? Has anyone yet been allowed to set out constructive charges against the ruling party? If accusations are made against the rulers with prima facie evidence, does that amount to speaking irresponsibly?

The Chief Minister cited Tiruvalluvar, saying: "He tells us to examine merits and faults, determine which predominate, and then reach a clear conclusion." Does Tamil Nadu not know what Ayyan Tiruvalluvar has suffered under this government? Was his portrait not removed from the textbook? Was the Tiruvalluvar statue in Kanyakumari not left without proper maintenance? Why, what condition is Valluvar Kottam in Chennai in today? Has it not been left dark and derelict? Were libraries across Tamil Nadu bearing Tiruvalluvar's name not pulled shut?

The Chief Minister also spoke in the Assembly of Perarignar Anna's greatness. Yet because the DMK government built in Chennai, for Anna's centenary, a library larger than any in Asia, what condition has it been reduced to today? Is there no limit to neglect and arrogance?

Jayalalithaa celebrated the Legislative Assembly's diamond jubilee in November 2012. Did she utter even one word then about Anna? Now that an election is approaching, she has mentioned Anna and C. Subramaniam in her Assembly speech!

In her reply to the debate on the Governor's Address, Jayalalithaa announced a five-percentage-point relaxation in the Teachers Eligibility Test for Scheduled Castes, Backward Classes, Most Backward Classes and persons with disabilities.

For several months, I, the Dravidar Kazhagam led by Tamilar Thalaivar K. Veeramani, and Tamil Nadu's opposition parties repeatedly urged this government to grant the appropriate concession to reservation categories. The government refused the demand. Instead, in one statement Jayalalithaa replied that reservation was already being provided in the Teachers Eligibility Test, without even understanding the basis of what we were asking. In my statement, I cited statistics showing that every other State provided this concession and asked why Tamil Nadu, the homeland of social justice, alone had failed to grant it.

The Tamil Nadu government nevertheless remained obstinate. Prince Gajendra Babu complained to the Chennai Regional Director of the National Commission for Scheduled Castes that reservation categories had not been granted a mark relaxation in the Teachers Eligibility Test. Acting on that complaint, the Commission ordered: "The Teachers Recruitment Board has acted against the reservation policy. Appropriate steps must be taken to grant a mark relaxation to reservation categories." Following that order, I wrote on 30 January 2014 under the title "Is There No Place for Reservation Under This Government?" It is because of the National Commission's order, and because I and leaders such as Dravidar Kazhagam president Veeramani persistently pressed the demand, that Jayalalithaa, left with no other course, has now climbed down and offered reservation categories a five-percentage-point relaxation in the Teachers Eligibility Test.

Jayalalithaa did not make this announcement out of genuine commitment to reservation policy. It is mere eyewash. Backward Classes, Most Backward Classes, Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes and persons with disabilities stand at different social and educational levels. How will members of these reservation categories accept Jayalalithaa's indiscriminate treatment of them all as one, granting everyone the same five-mark concession? Nor has she said anything about filling the backlog vacancies created by the Teachers Eligibility Test.

Jayalalithaa also spoke in the Assembly about industrial development. She boasted that Tamil Nadu had achieved manifold growth in new investment and employment. We have no objection to her feeling proud. But she has advanced the perverse argument that everything I say about investment is false. On 29 January 2014, under the title "Industrial Development in Tamil Nadu, Then and Now," I set out many details with evidence. Every newspaper reported that, to draw and attract Tamil Nadu's industrial investors, the Chief Minister of Karnataka entered our own State, came to Coimbatore on 20 January 2014, summoned industrialists and held a conference. As a result, about 200 industrialists operating in the Kongu-region districts of Coimbatore, Tiruppur, Erode and Salem came forward to invest ₹12,000 crore and establish industries in Chamarajanagar. Did that report appear only in Murasoli, or did it also appear in newspapers friendly to the ruling party?

When Karnataka's Chief Minister and officials came to Tamil Nadu, held a conference and invited investors, and Tamil Nadu's investors wholeheartedly accepted the invitation and pledged to establish industries in Karnataka, what were Tamil Nadu's Chief Minister and the ministers responsible for that department doing? After publicising that she was conducting the work of government from Kodanad, can she tell the Assembly that I was lying?

Is that all? A vice-chairman of the Union Government's Coir Board told reporters: "Although we came forward to create an industrial park on 200 acres at Vazhudareddy village in Villupuram, the Tamil Nadu government has still not granted permission to establish it"; and, "For the past two months, I have tried to meet Tamil Nadu's Rural Industries Minister about this, but have been unable to see him." Will Jayalalithaa attempt to say that this too is false?

I had asked about the Chief Minister's announcement of a scheme in the Assembly on 9 September 2011 under Rule 110, though the very same scheme had already appeared in the policy note placed before the Assembly on 25 August 2011. Was it not wrong for the Chief Minister to present an existing scheme to the House as a new announcement? After speaking in reply for two hours, should she not have answered this? She could answer only if she had an answer!

In the very reply Jayalalithaa read in the Assembly, she said: "Under the previous minority DMK government, the amount that fifty-four industrial enterprises were said to be investing through memoranda of understanding and government orders was ₹66,664 crore. Of these, twelve industries involving ₹15,982 crore never commenced." From this, has Jayalalithaa herself not admitted in the Assembly that forty-two industries involving ₹50,682 crore began under the DMK government? Is that not itself proof of industrial development during DMK rule?

She then said that from May 2011 until today, memoranda of understanding for investments of ₹26,625 crore had been signed with seventeen major Indian and multinational companies, potentially creating direct and indirect employment for 1.40 lakh people; and that investments of ₹10,660 crore had so far materialised, directly employing 10,022 people. The MoUs were for ₹26,625 crore, but only ₹10,660 crore in investment arrived. Barely forty per cent! Jayalalithaa herself admits that agreements were signed on the promise of jobs for 1.40 lakh people, but investment sufficient to employ only 10,022 people actually arrived. Is this the mark of industrial growth?

Even as Chief Minister Jayalalithaa claims that Tamil Nadu has achieved manifold growth in new industrial investment and job creation, Union Minister Sudarsana Natchiappan said in a special interview published on 4 February 2014: "The Union Industry Ministry has brought a massive industrial-development project to Tamil Nadu. The Union Government will lend up to ninety per cent of the investment required. The Tamil Nadu government has shown no interest in implementing it." He went on to point out the State government's lack of concern for industrial development. What answer will Jayalalithaa give him?

Chief Minister Jayalalithaa also presented some statistics on law and order. She said that the number of murders, 1,366 during her previous government in 2005, rose to 1,715 under the DMK government in 2010. Then, describing her own rule, she said there were 1,747 murders in 2011 and 1,806 in 2012. From Jayalalithaa's own figures, decide whether murders increased or decreased under AIADMK rule compared with DMK rule!
In Jayalalithaa's statistics on property-related cases, she stated that there were 18,585 cases in 2012 and 19,465 in 2013. Is 19,465 greater than 18,585, or less?

Jayalalithaa further claimed in her speech that the number of murders had fallen under her government. Do the people of Tamil Nadu not know how true that is? When we open the newspapers each day, is there a day without a murder? Police officials prepare reports claiming that such-and-such a percentage of crimes has been detected. Whichever government is in office, they furnish only statistics favourable to it. Chief Minister Jayalalithaa accepts them and boasts that the AIADMK government has detected this enormous percentage of crimes. One murder plainly before our eyes is that of Tiruchi Ramajayam. Did not even Subramanian Swamy issue a statement condemning the failure to maintain law and order in Tamil Nadu and the murders of several BJP leaders? How many days have passed since Pottu Suresh was murdered in Madurai? Have the culprits in all these murder cases been found?

Jayalalithaa told the Assembly: "As far as the government under my leadership is concerned, we have no intention of deceiving people by giving useless freebies. When I was Leader of the Opposition, I spoke in this very Assembly comparing those who deceive people with useless freebies to tricksters who fool children." But freebies are being distributed under this AIADMK government now. Is that not the conduct of tricksters who deceive children? If the DMK government distributed freebies, it was trickery; if the AIADMK government distributes them, is it praiseworthy?

As usual, Jayalalithaa did not fail to speak about Katchatheevu. It was Jayalalithaa who, after hoisting the flag at Fort St George in 1991, vowed: "I shall certainly retrieve Katchatheevu." Yet however many explanations and replies we give, she continues to heap accusations upon us. When the Government of India filed an affidavit in the Madras High Court stating that Indian fishermen had no right to fish near Katchatheevu, what objection did Jayalalithaa raise? Jayalalithaa, who writes to the Prime Minister every day, should she not condemn this action by the Union Government? Having failed to reply to the Prime Minister's letter about talks between Sri Lankan and Indian fishermen, Jayalalithaa has no moral right to speak either about the fishermen's issue or about Katchatheevu.

Jayalalithaa also said that Tamil Nadu would soon have no power cuts. Since the AIADMK came to office in 2011, how many times has she said, "Soon there will be no power cuts in Tamil Nadu"? Yet have the people of Tamil Nadu suffered from power cuts under any other government as they have under this one?

The truth is that even the electricity available now comes from projects initiated by the DMK government; not one new power project begun after the AIADMK took office has supplied electricity to Tamil Nadu. Moreover, many power projects announced under this government as involving investments of several thousand crore rupees have gone nowhere under the pretext of international tenders. In this splendid state of affairs, the Chief Minister has tried to divert attention by telling the Assembly that Tamil Nadu will soon have no power cuts.

Jayalalithaa also read passages in her speech about agriculture and livestock. Agriculture has failed to yield proper returns during all three years of this government. Even the relief the government promised farmers was not properly paid. This government did not even provide compensation for livestock killed by foot-and-mouth disease. Let the people of Tamil Nadu decide whether what the Chief Minister of an AIADMK government that has inflicted successive troubles, sorrows, anguish and calamities upon the great farming community and every other section of society delivered in response to the Governor's Address was a reply, or an exercise in blame!
With affection,
M. K.
6 February 2014
